note: Concept-level cards for oral examination. Filter by 'topic' in the app.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What is cloud computing?
flashcards.answer: A model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction. It is essentially the realisation of utility computing - you pay only for what you use, with no investment in building or maintaining the infrastructure.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What does 'ubiquitous' mean in the cloud definition, and does resource location still matter?
flashcards.answer: Servers and data can be located anywhere and be accessed from anywhere. But location still matters in practice: the distance between the customer and the server/data affects perceived performance and quality of experience, and some consumers need control over location for legal or compliance reasons - which is why providers let you specify location at a higher abstraction level (country, region, datacenter) rather than not at all.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: List the five essential characteristics of cloud computing.
flashcards.answer: On-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service. A capability must satisfy all five to be a cloud service - failing even one disqualifies it.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What are the two options for satisfying on-demand self-service, and why does the distinction matter?
flashcards.answer: Option A: fully automated service provisioning. Option B: the consumer uses an automated interface to request and track the service, but the provider may still use manual labour to actually provision it. The distinction matters because Option B hides a human in the provisioning path - which is usually what also breaks rapid elasticity, since manual provisioning cannot be rapid by definition.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What is resource pooling, and what does the multi-tenant model require?
flashcards.answer: Computing resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers, with physical and virtual resources dynamically assigned and reassigned according to demand. The multi-tenant model means multiple tenants access the same application logic simultaneously, each with its own view of the application as if it were a dedicated instance and unaware of the others, while the application isolates each tenant's data and configuration. It must guarantee usage isolation (performance and availability), data security, recovery from failures, safe upgrades, scalability, metered usage, and data-tier isolation.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: Explain the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling.
flashcards.answer: Horizontal: scaling out (adding more instances of a resource, e.g. more servers in a cluster) and scaling in (removing them). Vertical: scaling up (increasing the capacity of a single resource, e.g. more CPU or RAM on one server) and scaling down.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What is elasticity, and how does it relate to scalability?
flashcards.answer: The degree to which a system adapts to workload changes by automatically provisioning and deprovisioning resources, so that at each point in time the available resources match current demand as closely as possible. Scalability is the underlying ability to sustain more load by adding resources - it is a prerequisite, but it says nothing about time. Elasticity adds the temporal dimension: how fast, how often, and at what granularity the adaptation happens.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: Why isn't manually adding VMs enough to satisfy rapid elasticity? What's needed instead?
flashcards.answer: With manual provisioning you can scale, but you cannot be rapid in following demand - a human has to notice and react. Rapid elasticity requires automation: continuously monitoring a metric (e.g. CPU usage), and automatically acting on a rule such as 'if CPU usage exceeds 70% for 1 minute, add 1 VM'. With multiple VMs the decision is taken on the aggregate (e.g. average CPU). This is what auto-scaling plus elastic load balancing provides.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What is measured service, and what two things does it enable?
flashcards.answer: Cloud service characteristics, including resource usage, are monitored in enough detail to support the consumer's requirements. It enables proper billing (per hour/minute/second of a VM, MB of storage or I/O, GB transferred) and SLA management - guaranteeing and verifying commitments like 99.9% uptime, a given bandwidth, or a certain CPU share. Consumers also need it to automate management: autoscaling thresholds, cost alerts, application-level SLAs, and resiliency.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What is resiliency, and give examples of cloud mechanisms that provide it.
flashcards.answer: The ability to provide and maintain an acceptable level of service in the face of faults and challenges to normal operation. Examples: an elastic load balancer that automatically excludes a failed node from the pool; autoscaling configured with a minimum number of servers that must stay up despite failures; container orchestration (Kubernetes, Swarm) maintaining a desired number of running containers despite failures. All of these depend on measured/monitored services.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: How does the level of control and responsibility change as you move from IaaS to SaaS?
flashcards.answer: IaaS: the consumer controls operating systems, storage, and deployed applications, plus possibly limited control of select networking components. PaaS: the consumer controls deployed applications and possibly configuration of the hosting environment. SaaS: the consumer controls only user-specific application settings. Moving IaaS to SaaS, consumer control over resources decreases, the expertise needed decreases, and ease of use increases.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: In the cloud shared-responsibility model, what is the provider responsible for versus the customer?
flashcards.answer: Provider - security OF the cloud: physical security of datacenters with controlled need-based access, hardware and software infrastructure (storage decommissioning, host OS access logging and auditing), network infrastructure (intrusion detection), and virtualization infrastructure (instance isolation). Customer - security IN the cloud: the VM instance operating system including patching and maintenance, the applications, passwords and role-based access, OS/host-based firewalls and intrusion detection, network configuration, and account management with per-user login and permission settings.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What are the four deployment models, and what defines each?
flashcards.answer: Public: provisioned for open use by the general public, owned and operated by a provider - 'all you can access'. Private: dedicated entirely to a specific customer, in logical or physical isolation from other customers, offering maximum control and security (on-premise or as a virtual private cloud). Hybrid: a combination of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures, e.g. mixing private and public. Community: shared by several organisations supporting a specific community with shared concerns, e.g. government agencies or collaborating research institutions.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What is a Virtual Private Cloud, and how do different providers' offerings differ?
flashcards.answer: A private-cloud-like environment carved out inside a public cloud. Some providers focus on VPN features to securely connect your resources within the public cloud environment; others instead offer environments with greater physical isolation. It's the pragmatic middle ground between a true private cloud and a plain public one.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: How do you determine whether a computing capability is actually offered as a cloud service?
flashcards.answer: Using three worksheets. The Cloud Service Worksheet confirms whether it is a cloud service at all, by checking it against the five essential characteristics. The Cloud Service Model Worksheet determines which service model it uses (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS). The Cloud Deployment Model Worksheet determines which deployment model it uses.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: Name the five actors in the cloud reference architecture and what each does.
flashcards.answer: Cloud Consumer: the individual or organisation using the services. Cloud Provider: the entity making the services available. Cloud Auditor: an independent party assessing the security, privacy, and performance of the services. Cloud Broker: an intermediary that manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services and negotiates relationships between providers and consumers. Cloud Carrier: the intermediary providing connectivity and transport of services from providers to consumers.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What is a Cloud Broker, and what are its three categories of service?
flashcards.answer: A middleman between the consumer and multiple cloud providers: rather than the consumer buying and managing services directly from several companies, the broker aggregates, integrates, or customizes them into a single cohesive package. Service Intermediation: enhancing a service by adding value - extra security features, performance monitoring, billing. Service Aggregation: combining services from different providers into a unified offering, simplifying management. Service Arbitrage: dynamically selecting services from different providers based on cost, performance, or availability.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What is a Cloud Auditor, and why would a consumer rely on one?
flashcards.answer: An independent third-party organisation that objectively evaluates a provider's services, focusing on security protocols, privacy standards, and performance metrics. It exists so the consumer can verify the provider is actually delivering what it promised and adhering to legal regulations - e.g. a bank moving sensitive financial data has an independent auditor run a security check and issue a certification report, which the bank reviews before signing the contract.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What is Service Orchestration in the reference architecture, and what does it look like at each service layer?
flashcards.answer: The composition, arrangement, coordination, and management of system components and computing resources so the provider can deliver cloud services to consumers. It maps onto three layers: the Service Layer (SaaS/PaaS/IaaS interfaces the provider exposes), the Resource Abstraction and Control Layer (the software fabric - hypervisors creating virtual machines, storage, network abstractions), and the Physical Resource Layer (hardware and facilities). The orchestration task differs by model: IaaS orchestrates VMs and virtual devices, PaaS typically orchestrates containers (e.g. Kubernetes), SaaS orchestrates microservices and SOA.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: Which architectural layer makes the five essential characteristics possible, and how?
flashcards.answer: The Resource Abstraction and Control Layer. Its control software handles resource allocation and usage monitoring, which is what directly enables Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, and Measured Service.

flashcards.topic: Lecture 1 - Cloud Definition & NIST Reference Architecture
flashcards.question: What is Cloud Service Management, and what are its three operational perspectives?
flashcards.answer: All the service-related functions necessary for managing and operating the services required by or proposed to cloud consumers. Business Support: client-facing business services - customer, contract, and inventory management, accounting and billing, reporting and auditing, pricing and rating. Provisioning/Configuration: technical delivery and lifecycle management - rapid provisioning, resource change, monitoring and reporting, metering, SLA management. Portability/Interoperability: capabilities designed to prevent vendor lock-in - portability is the ability to move applications and data from one provider to another without significant rework, interoperability is the ability of different cloud services and platforms to work together seamlessly.

flashcards.question: What is a distributed system? Give the two complementary definitions.
flashcards.answer: One view: a collection of independent computers that appears to its users as a single coherent system - this emphasizes unified usage and the aggregation of distributed resources. The other: a system in which components located at networked computers communicate and coordinate their actions only by passing messages - this emphasizes the networked nature and message-passing communication.

flashcards.question: How does distributed computing differ from a distributed system?
flashcards.answer: A distributed system is an architecture; distributed computing is a computational model - it defines HOW the computation is carried out. The computation is broken into units executed concurrently on different computing elements: cores within the same processor, processors on the same computer, or processors on different nodes. Being distributed also usually implies different physical locations (giving reliability and fault tolerance - if one location loses power, computation continues elsewhere) and heterogeneity in hardware and software.

flashcards.question: Why is cloud computing considered a specialized form of distributed computing?
flashcards.answer: Because it is a distributed system that adds utilization models for remotely provisioning scalable and measured resources - the service model layer. The layers map directly: Applications correspond to SaaS, Middleware to PaaS, and OS plus Hardware to IaaS.

flashcards.question: What does an architectural style define?
flashcards.answer: The different roles of components in the distributed system, how components are distributed across multiple machines, and what connectors (communication mechanisms) are used. It describes a family of systems as a pattern of structural organisation, providing a vocabulary of components and connectors plus constraints on how they can be combined.

flashcards.question: What is the pipe-and-filter architectural style? Give an example.
flashcards.answer: A style where data flows through a chain of processing stages, each transforming it and passing it on. MapReduce is the canonical example - a word count can be distributed across map and reduce stages connected as a pipeline.

flashcards.question: What characterizes an event-based system?
flashcards.answer: Components are loosely coupled and connected through an event/message broker: each component publishes a collection of events that other components can subscribe to, and a subscriber provides a callback executed when the event fires. This fosters open systems - a new component can be integrated easily as long as it has compliant interfaces for registering to events.

flashcards.question: In client/server architecture, what are the three main components and the difference between thin-client and fat-client?
flashcards.answer: The three components are presentation, application logic, and data storage. Thin-client: the client has only presentation, so the server does all the work (application logic plus data storage). Fat-client: the client also handles some application logic, leaving the server with data storage.

flashcards.question: Why does the two-tier client/server model scale poorly compared to three-tier or N-tier?
flashcards.answer: Two-tier is the classic model and is suitable only for systems of limited size - it suffers from scalability issues because everything sits on essentially two nodes. Three-tier and N-tier are more scalable because the conceptual tiers can be distributed across several computing nodes, which also lets performance bottlenecks be isolated.

flashcards.question: What is the peer-to-peer architectural style, and when is it suitable?
flashcards.answer: A symmetric architecture where all components (peers) play the same role and incorporate both client and server capabilities. It suits highly decentralized architectures and scales better along the dimension of number of peers - used for file-sharing applications and distributed/cloud storage systems.

flashcards.question: What is a service in SOA?
flashcards.answer: A logical representation of a repeatable business activity with a specified outcome (e.g. check customer credit, provide weather data). It is self-contained/autonomous, may be composed of other services, and appears as a black box to its consumers. An application is then an aggregation of services coordinated within the SOA.

flashcards.question: What are the four main characteristics of a service?
flashcards.answer: Explicit boundaries: interfaces are kept minimal to foster reuse and simplify interaction. Autonomy: services can be integrated into different systems at the same time and can handle failures. Shared schema and contracts: a contract describes the structure of messages the service can send/receive plus constraints on their ordering, allowing structural compatibility to be checked by machine. Compatibility based on policy: policies define capabilities and requirements as expressions that must hold true, determining semantic compatibility.

flashcards.question: What are the three roles in SOA, and how do they interact?
flashcards.answer: Service Provider: maintains the service and publishes it in a registry along with its schema and contract. Service Consumer: locates the service metadata in the registry and develops the client components to bind and use it. Service Registry: stores the service descriptors. Interaction: 1) the consumer discovers a service in the registry, 2) the registry returns binding information, 3) the consumer establishes a connection with the provider using that binding info, 4) the consumer uses the service. Note a component can play both provider and consumer roles, aggregating services into workflows.

flashcards.question: What are the main benefits of SOA?
flashcards.answer: Service re-use; message monitoring, control, and transformation; complex event processing; service composition; service discovery.

flashcards.question: Explain the difference between service orchestration and service choreography, with an example of each.
flashcards.answer: Orchestration: a single centralized executable process (the orchestrator) coordinates interaction among services and is responsible for invoking and combining them - e.g. a travel planner running select-destination, display-hotels, select-hotel, suggest-options as one workflow. Choreography: a global description of participating services defined by message exchange, rules of interaction, and agreements between endpoints, with no single point of control - e.g. an online credit-card payment where the Seller Transaction Service sends a request to the Credit Card company Service which accepts/denies and replies, or an authentication flow where a Client Service and Auth Service exchange requests including a possible three-factor step.

flashcards.question: What is a microservice architecture?
flashcards.answer: An architectural style that structures an application as a collection of services that are highly maintainable and testable, loosely coupled, independently deployable, organized around business capabilities, and owned by a small team. It enables rapid, frequent, reliable delivery of large complex applications and lets an organisation evolve its technology stack.

flashcards.question: Explain the three axes of the scale cube.
flashcards.answer: X-axis: horizontal duplication - going from one instance to many identical instances. Y-axis: functional decomposition - going from a monolith to microservices. Z-axis: data partitioning - going from one partition to many. Microservices are what create the Y-axis dimension of scalability.

flashcards.question: What are the pros and cons of a monolithic architecture?
flashcards.answer: Pros: simple to develop and to make radical changes to, straightforward to test and deploy, and easy to scale - but only horizontally, and even that is limited because a shared database becomes a bottleneck and all application functionality gets replicated whether it needs to be or not. Cons: as complexity increases and technologies evolve, development, testing, deployment, and scaling all become much more difficult. In production terms, a huge team works on a single codebase, creating queues and bottlenecks due to complex testing - whereas in microservices small teams work on separate repositories with automated deployment pipelines.

flashcards.question: Compare SOA and microservices on taxonomy.
flashcards.answer: SOA has many abstract layers - business services, messaging middleware, enterprise services, application services, infrastructure services - and requires a high level of coordination between different teams to handle a single request. Microservices have only an API layer plus functional and infrastructure services (e.g. Auth, Login, Monitoring), so a single team manages the whole service without external coordination.

flashcards.question: Compare SOA and microservices on service ownership and granularity.
flashcards.answer: Ownership: SOA requires a high coordination effort among teams; microservices require little to no coordination, since each team owns its service end to end. Granularity: microservices components are generally single-purpose, specialized services; SOA components range from small application services to very large enterprise services - it's common for one SOA component to be an entire product or subsystem. Granularity impacts performance and transaction management (ACID vs BASE).

flashcards.question: Compare SOA and microservices on component sharing, and explain bounded context.
flashcards.answer: SOA follows share-as-much-as-possible, which leads to tightly coupled components and increases the overall risk associated with change. Microservices follow share-as-little-as-possible, using a bounded context: a component and its associated data form a single closed unit with minimal dependencies, self-contained and exposing only a well-defined interface and contract. This can violate the Don't-Repeat-Yourself principle, but it makes maintenance and deployment drastically easier and safer - less code to deploy, less risk a change to one service affects others, and more robust applications with fewer side effects.

flashcards.question: Compare SOA and microservices on orchestration versus choreography.
flashcards.answer: Microservices favour choreography over orchestration, primarily because the architecture topology lacks a centralized middleware component. But too much choreography leads to efferent coupling - if a service depends on many other services the system becomes too rigid. SOA relies on both orchestration and choreography.

flashcards.question: Compare SOA and microservices on middleware versus API layer.
flashcards.answer: SOA relies on messaging middleware (an integration bus) to coordinate service calls, which also provides mediation and routing, message enhancement, message transformation, and protocol transformation - letting it integrate incompatible services. Microservices do not support messaging middleware at all; instead they use an API layer in front of the services acting as a service-access facade. It abstracts endpoint locations away from consumers and allows the granularity of a service to change without impacting them.

flashcards.question: Compare SOA and microservices on application scope and heterogeneous interoperability.
flashcards.answer: Application scope (the overall size of application the pattern can support): SOA suits large, complex, enterprise-wide systems requiring integration with many heterogeneous applications and services, especially with components shared across the enterprise; microservices suit smaller, well-partitioned web-based systems. Interoperability: SOA accepts essentially any protocol through its middleware and can integrate systems in different languages and platforms with no upper limit; microservices deliberately simplify by reducing the number of choices for service integration - REST and simple messaging are the most common.

flashcards.question: What is containerization, and how does it differ from virtualization?
flashcards.answer: Operating-system-level virtualization - it leverages multiprogramming techniques at the OS level. There is no virtual machine manager or hypervisor and no hardware emulation: all containers share the same host OS. In virtualization, by contrast, a hypervisor manages the relationship between the system and the VMs it hosts, each with its own OS.

flashcards.question: What three mechanisms is containerization built on, and what does each control?
flashcards.answer: Namespaces - what a process can see and use (isolation). Cgroups - how much a process can use (resource limiting). UnionFS - merging physically separate filesystems into a single logical view.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between application containers and system containers?
flashcards.answer: Application containers hold a single process, use a layered filesystem, run microservices or applications, and are used for distributing applications (e.g. Docker, CRI-O). System containers hold an entire operating system, are filesystem-neutral, run like a lightweight virtual machine, and are used for providing underlying infrastructure (e.g. LXC/LXD, BSD jails).

flashcards.question: What is chroot, and how does it relate to containers?
flashcards.answer: Chroot changes the filesystem root directory for a process and its children to a specific directory, so they cannot access any part of the filesystem outside that new root. Since Unix systems also expose devices as part of the filesystem, this can completely isolate a set of processes. Containerization is the evolution of this idea - though modern container runtimes no longer use chroot itself, having replaced it with pivot_root.

flashcards.question: What is a namespace, and what kinds of resources can it isolate?
flashcards.answer: It wraps a global system resource in an abstraction so that processes inside the namespace perceive they have their own isolated instance of that resource. Changes to the resource are visible to other processes in the namespace and invisible outside it. Namespace types cover cgroup root directory, IPC (System V IPC, POSIX message queues), network (devices, stacks, ports), mount points, PIDs, user/group IDs, and UTS (hostname and NIS domain).

flashcards.question: Which system calls create, join, and move a process between namespaces?
flashcards.answer: clone() - when a process is created, an argument specifies one or more namespaces to create. setns() - lets a process join an already existing namespace. unshare() - moves a process's context into a new namespace.

flashcards.question: What is the goal of cgroups, and how does the kernel expose them?
flashcards.answer: To support resource limiting, prioritization, accounting, and controlling, by allowing processes to be organized into hierarchical groups. The kernel exposes the cgroup interface through a pseudo-filesystem called cgroupfs, with different controllers for different resources (one for CPU, one for memory, one for PIDs, and so on).

flashcards.question: What is UnionFS, and how does branch precedence resolve conflicts?
flashcards.answer: Unioning keeps files physically separate but merges them logically into a single view; each physical directory in the union is called a branch, and each branch is assigned a precedence. If the same FILE exists in two branches, the union shows the contents and attributes from the higher-precedence branch and ignores the lower one. If the same DIRECTORY exists in two branches, the union shows a combination of both, with duplicate entries automatically removed.

flashcards.question: How does UnionFS let you write to a read-only branch?
flashcards.answer: Through copy-on-write unions: UnionFS can mix read-only and read-write branches, and the union as a whole is read-write. Copy-on-write semantics give the illusion you can modify files and directories on read-only branches while keeping the base intact. When a read-only file is opened for writing, it is copied up ('copyup') to a higher-priority read-write branch, with any needed parent directories created automatically, and all writes actually happen there.

flashcards.question: Describe Docker's architecture and how its parts communicate.
flashcards.answer: The Docker engine is a client/server application. The client is the CLI, through which the user interacts; the server is the Docker daemon, which is responsible for physically creating and managing Docker objects (images, containers, networks, data volumes). They communicate through the Docker REST API, which can also be called separately by applications rather than through the CLI. Containers run directly on the host's kernel, whether that host is physical or virtual.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between a Docker image and a Docker container?
flashcards.answer: An image is a read-only template containing the instructions for creating a container, organised in layers defined by a Dockerfile; images are downloaded from a registry. A container is a runnable instance of an image, managed via the CLI or the Docker API - it adds a writable layer on top of the read-only image layers.

flashcards.question: Walk through what happens when you run 'docker run -i -t ubuntu /bin/bash'.
flashcards.answer: The daemon locates the ubuntu image and downloads it if not stored locally; creates a new container; allocates a read/write filesystem as the final layer on top of the image, isolated from the host filesystem; creates a network interface connected to the default network; then starts the container and runs the /bin/bash command.

flashcards.question: What is the container writable layer, and what are its drawbacks?
flashcards.answer: The read/write layer added on top of the image when a container is created. Data written there does not persist once the container terminates; it is tightly coupled to the host the container runs on, which reduces portability; and performance is reduced.

flashcards.question: What is a Docker volume, and what are its advantages?
flashcards.answer: Storage created and managed by Docker, isolated from the host and stored in a specific directory on the Docker host; it can be mounted into multiple containers simultaneously, read/write or read-only. Advantages: easier to back up or migrate than bind mounts; can be more safely shared among multiple containers; volume drivers let you store data on remote hosts or cloud providers, encrypt contents, or add other functionality; new volumes can be pre-populated by a container; they work on both Linux and Windows. The tradeoff is that they must be managed through the Docker CLI or API - you cannot access the data directly like a normal filesystem.

flashcards.question: What is a bind mount, and how does it compare to a volume?
flashcards.answer: A file or directory anywhere on the host machine, referenced by its full host path, mounted into a container (created by the container if it doesn't exist). It is shared with host processes. It is NOT portable, since it depends on the host filesystem - but performance is usually higher than volumes, and it gives direct filesystem-level access that volumes don't.

flashcards.question: What is a tmpfs mount, and what are its limitations?
flashcards.answer: An area of memory outside the container's writable layer - either in the container namespace or shared with the host. It is temporary, persisted only in host memory, and removed when the container stops. Limitations: available only on Linux hosts, and not shareable among containers.

flashcards.question: Choose the storage option for each: (a) back up/migrate data between Docker hosts, (b) share config files from host to container, (c) data must not persist anywhere for security reasons, (d) share source code between a dev environment and a container.
flashcards.answer: (a) Volume - also the choice for sharing data among multiple running containers, when there's no guarantee about the host's directory structure, or to store data on a remote host/cloud provider. (b) Bind mount. (c) Tmpfs - also for manipulating large volumes of non-persistent data with high performance. (d) Bind mount - also correct when the host's file/directory structure is guaranteed to match what the container requires.

flashcards.question: Name the Docker network drivers and what each is for.
flashcards.answer: Bridge: the default driver, used when applications run in standalone containers that need to communicate on the same host. Host: for standalone containers, removes network isolation between container and host so the container uses the host's networking directly. Overlay: connects multiple Docker daemons together so swarm services (or containers on different hosts) can communicate, removing the need for OS-level routing between them. Macvlan: assigns a MAC address to the container so it appears as a physical device on the network, with the daemon routing traffic by MAC - the best choice for legacy applications expecting a direct physical network connection. None: disables all networking for the container.

flashcards.question: Choose the network driver for each: (a) migrating from a VM setup, (b) the network stack should not be isolated from the host, (c) containers on different Docker hosts must communicate, (d) multiple containers must communicate on the same host.
flashcards.answer: (a) Macvlan - also the answer when containers need to look like physical hosts with a unique MAC address. (b) Host. (c) Overlay. (d) User-defined bridge network.

flashcards.question: What are the three characteristics virtualization guarantees?
flashcards.answer: Increased security, managed execution, and portability. The environment consists of a virtualization layer - a hypervisor or virtual machine monitor (VMM) - sitting between the physical hardware (host) and the virtual machine (guest).

flashcards.question: Why does virtualization increase security?
flashcards.answer: The VMM controls and filters the activity of the guest, preventing harmful operations from being performed. Resources exposed by the host can be hidden or protected from the guest, so sensitive information on the host is naturally hidden without installing complex security policies. Examples: a Java applet from the internet runs in a sandboxed JVM that filters harmful instructions based on security policies; in hardware virtualization the filesystem exposed to the VM is completely separated from the host's.

flashcards.question: What is managed execution as a virtualization characteristic?
flashcards.answer: Physical resources are converted into virtual resources through virtualization techniques - sharing, aggregation, emulation, and isolation.

flashcards.question: Why does virtualization increase portability?
flashcards.answer: Portability is the ability to transfer and use data and applications from one computing platform to another. Virtualized artifacts are decoupled from the specific physical platform: Java or Python code runs anywhere with a matching VM; a Docker container runs on any platform with the Docker engine; a VM boots from an image stored in a specific disk format (vdi, vhd, vmdk, ami, and so on), so you can move VMs around keeping or converting their format.

flashcards.question: Describe the layered structure of a computer system relevant to virtualization.
flashcards.answer: Applications sit on top; they reach Libraries via the API. Libraries communicate with the Operating System through the Application Binary Interface (ABI). The Operating System communicates with the Hardware through the Instruction Set Architecture (ISA). Applications can also reach the ISA directly (the User ISA). Understanding this stack is what lets you see where each virtualization technique intervenes.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between privileged and non-privileged instructions, and what are the two kinds of sensitive instruction?
flashcards.answer: Non-privileged instructions can be used without interfering with other tasks - e.g. arithmetic and floating-point instructions; this ring is user mode. Privileged instructions execute under specific restrictions and are mostly used for sensitive operations; they require supervisor/kernel mode. Sensitive instructions come in two kinds: behavior-sensitive, which operate on I/O, and control-sensitive, which alter the state of the CPU registers.

flashcards.question: Where does the hypervisor execute, and what is its core challenge?
flashcards.answer: It runs in supervisor mode - the most privileged level (its name comes from conceptually sitting on top of supervisor mode). Its core challenge is to fully emulate and manage the CPU state for guest operating systems: all sensitive instructions should execute in privileged mode, which requires supervisor mode.

flashcards.question: Why was the x86 architecture originally not virtualizable, and how was it fixed?
flashcards.answer: Because sensitive instructions were not a subset of privileged instructions - a set of sensitive instructions could be called in user mode without trapping, so there was no isolation among guest OSes and the trap mechanism couldn't be relied on. Intel VT-x and later AMD-V fixed this by redesigning those instructions as privileged, so they trap to the hypervisor properly.

flashcards.question: Distinguish Type I and Type II hypervisors.
flashcards.answer: Type I (native/bare-metal): the hypervisor sits directly on the hardware and uses ISA calls to communicate with it - e.g. ESX/vSphere, Xen, Hyper-V. Type II (hosted): the hypervisor runs on top of a host OS and uses system calls to communicate with it - e.g. VMware Workstation/Fusion, VirtualBox, KVM, Parallels.

flashcards.question: What three properties must a Virtual Machine Manager satisfy?
flashcards.answer: Equivalence: a guest running under the VMM's control should exhibit the same behaviour as when executed directly on the physical host. Resource control: the VMM should be in complete control of the virtualized resources. Efficiency: a statistically dominant fraction of the machine instructions should execute without any intervention from the VMM.

flashcards.question: What are the three modules of a VMM, and what does each do?
flashcards.answer: Dispatcher: the entry point of the monitor - it reroutes instructions issued by the VM instance to one of the other two modules. Allocator: decides which system resources are provided to the VM; whenever a VM tries to execute an instruction that would change its allocated resources, the dispatcher invokes the allocator. Interpreter: routines executed whenever the VM executes a privileged instruction - a trap is triggered and the corresponding routine runs.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between a microkernel and a monolithic Type I hypervisor?
flashcards.answer: A microkernel VMM includes only memory and CPU management, leaving device drivers in the host OS (e.g. Hyper-V, Xen). A monolithic VMM includes all of that itself, device drivers included (e.g. VMware ESX).

flashcards.question: In the Xen architecture, what does the VMM implement and what is Domain 0 responsible for?
flashcards.answer: The Xen VMM implements memory management, CPU management, and device I/O access, and provides hypercalls for sensitive instructions - which requires modifying the guest OS, i.e. paravirtualization. Domain 0 is a privileged OS that performs VM management: allocating and mapping resources for itself, and creating, copying, saving, reading, modifying, sharing, and migrating VMs.

flashcards.question: How does the hypervisor handle a trap on a privileged instruction?
flashcards.answer: The trap fires when a guest tries to execute an instruction reserved for kernel mode. The hypervisor inspects the instruction and checks where it came from: if it came from the guest OS, it arranges for the instruction to actually be carried out safely; if it came from a user program, it emulates what real hardware would do when confronted with that sensitive instruction executed in user mode.

flashcards.question: What is full virtualization?
flashcards.answer: The VMM scans the instruction stream: non-critical instructions run directly on the hardware without the VMM, while privileged, control-sensitive, and behavior-sensitive instructions are identified, trapped into the VMM, and emulated - via binary translation or hypercalls. The guest OS is completely decoupled from the underlying hardware and is unaware that it is being virtualized.

flashcards.question: What is binary translation, and what is a basic block?
flashcards.answer: A technique for handling non-virtualizable architectures: the hypervisor analyses the OS code block by block looking for sensitive instructions, and replaces any it finds with a safe call to a hypervisor procedure that handles them. It also replaces the branch at the end of the block with a call into the hypervisor so the procedure repeats for the next block. A basic block is a short, straight-line sequence of instructions ending with a branch - containing no jump, call, trap, return, or other flow-alteration instruction.

flashcards.question: Why isn't dynamic binary translation as expensive as it sounds?
flashcards.answer: Translated blocks are cached, so if the hypervisor encounters the same block again it doesn't retranslate it. Most code blocks contain no sensitive or privileged instructions and can execute natively. And the translator can ignore user processes entirely - they run in user mode, where the hardware ignores sensitive instructions anyway. Only guest OS code needs full translation, precisely to avoid traps, which are expensive to handle.

flashcards.question: What did hardware-supported virtualization change, and what is the tradeoff versus binary translation?
flashcards.answer: It made sensitive instructions a proper subset of privileged instructions, so I/O and similar instructions trap to the hypervisor, which does the work on the VM's behalf. The tradeoff: a large number of traps can paradoxically slow performance down compared to an excellent binary translator, since each trap carries real overhead.

flashcards.question: What is paravirtualization, and what does it require?
flashcards.answer: The guest OS knows it is running in a VM, so instead of pretending to be hardware, the hypervisor exposes an API that the guest OS calls. Non-virtualizable/sensitive instructions are replaced with hypercalls by an intelligent compiler. It requires the guest OS to support paravirtualization, and needs hardware-assisted virtualization to prevent performance losses. It leverages a microkernel architecture handling base elements like processes, interrupts, and scheduling. Where full virtualization has libraries talking to emulated hardware and causing traps, paravirtualization uses specific libraries that launch hypercalls directly - much lighter. Examples: KVM, Hyper-V.

flashcards.question: What is the critical difference between autonomic computing and automation?
flashcards.answer: Autonomic computing aims to keep humans out of the loop entirely - they only provide high-level objectives. Automation (IT operations automation) aims to reduce human interaction and manual processes, but doesn't necessarily remove the human from the decision path.

flashcards.question: What does IT operations automation do, and what are its main benefits?
flashcards.answer: It uses software tools and technologies to automate organizational and operational IT processes: automating repetitive tasks, orchestrating workflows, and integrating automation into existing systems and applications. Benefits: improved efficiency and productivity - reducing the time and effort of manual tasks frees IT staff for more strategic, value-added activities; reduced human error - eliminating mistakes caused by manual intervention gives more accurate, consistent results; saved time and costs - minimizing the burden of repetitive tasks lowers overall operational costs.

flashcards.question: What is Ansible, and what does it mean that it is agentless?
flashcards.answer: An open source IT configuration management, deployment, and orchestration tool. Agentless means no software needs to be installed on the remote machines to make them manageable, so there is no overhead when management isn't running. This makes it ideal for high-security environments or high-performance cases where there are concerns about the stability or permanence of a management agent.

flashcards.question: What is Ansible's push model, and what happens with nodes that can't run code?
flashcards.answer: Control instructions are prepared and stored on the control server and then pushed to the managed hosts, using remote management frameworks that already exist on the platform - SSH for Linux/UNIX, WinRM for Windows. The pushed code is called a module. Remote machines cannot see or affect how other machines are configured. Not all nodes can run code - network devices, for instance - so for those the module is executed locally on the Ansible control node instead; on Linux or Windows nodes the module is copied over, executed, and then deleted.

flashcards.question: What are Ansible Playbooks, and what can they do?
flashcards.answer: YAML-syntax files allowing simple configuration management and multi-machine deployment; they are repeatable and reusable. They can declare configurations; orchestrate the steps of any manual ordered process across multiple sets of machines in a defined order; launch tasks synchronously (default) or asynchronously (for long-running tasks); define the system's desired state; and be idempotent - applying them more than once doesn't change the state further.

flashcards.question: In Ansible, what is the relationship between plays, tasks, modules, and the inventory?
flashcards.answer: A playbook consists of a series of plays. Each play defines automation across a set of hosts - the inventory - and consists of multiple tasks. A task can target one, many, or all hosts in the inventory, and is a call to an Ansible module: a small piece of code doing one specific thing, from simple (placing a configuration file, installing a package) to complex (spinning up an entire cloud infrastructure).

flashcards.question: Why are Ansible core modules written to check before acting?
flashcards.answer: They are written to allow easy configuration of a desired state: the module checks whether the specified task actually needs to be done before executing it - e.g. a task defined to start a webserver only acts if the webserver isn't already running. This desired-state approach means configuration can be applied repeatedly without side effects, and runs quickly and efficiently when it has already been applied.

flashcards.question: How does Ansible implement each of the four self-* properties, and where does it sit in the MAPE paradigm?
flashcards.answer: Ansible represents the Execute phase of MAPE. Self-configuration: once a reconfiguration is decided, a playbook deploys the new system configuration. Self-optimization: once adding a new server is decided, a playbook performs all the required actions. Self-healing: if a server or application is degrading, a playbook that detaches and restarts it can be executed. Self-protection: if the system is under attack due to a vulnerability, a playbook rolling out an update can be deployed.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between automation and orchestration, and in what contexts does orchestration appear?
flashcards.answer: Automation is setting up one task to run on its own. Orchestration is automating a series of individual tasks so they work together as a single process or workflow. It appears in three contexts: service orchestration, IT infrastructure orchestration, and container orchestration.

flashcards.question: What is container orchestration, and what features does it typically offer?
flashcards.answer: It allows cloud and application providers to define how to select, deploy, monitor, and dynamically control the configuration of multi-container applications in the cloud - it concerns runtime management, supporting the deploy, run, and maintain phases. Typical features: resource limit control, scheduling, load balancing, health check, fault tolerance, and auto-scaling. Note the split of responsibility: a container manager provides APIs covering at least the acquire-to-run phases, while the orchestrator automates the deploy, run, and maintain phases.

flashcards.question: What does Kubernetes actually do - what does 'managing containerized workloads' mean?
flashcards.answer: Service discovery and load balancing; storage orchestration (automatically mounting storage); automated rollouts and rollbacks (automatically bringing deployed containers to a described desired state); automatic bin packing (fitting containers onto cluster nodes to make best use of resources while giving each the CPU and memory it needs); self-healing (restarting, replacing, killing, and not advertising faulty containers); and secret and configuration management.

flashcards.question: How is a Kubernetes cluster structured?
flashcards.answer: Into two parts. The Control Plane - the brain of the system, running on a dedicated server or VM - contains kube-apiserver, etcd, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, and cloud-controller-manager. The Nodes - each a VM or physical server - run the actual containerized workload (Pods) and contain kubelet, kube-proxy, and a container runtime.

flashcards.question: What are the Kubernetes control plane components and what does each do?
flashcards.answer: kube-apiserver: the front-end of the control plane; it scales horizontally. etcd: a consistent, highly-available key-value store used as the backing store for all cluster data. kube-scheduler: watches for newly created Pods with no assigned node and selects a node for them. kube-controller-manager: runs the controller processes (control loops). cloud-controller-manager: links the cluster to a specific cloud provider's API.

flashcards.question: What are the Kubernetes node components and what does each do?
flashcards.answer: kubelet: the primary node agent running on each node, ensuring the containers described in a PodSpec are running and healthy; it can also register the node with the API server. kube-proxy: a network proxy running on each node that maintains network rules allowing communication to the cluster's Pods from sessions inside or outside the cluster. Container runtime: the software responsible for actually running containers, e.g. Docker or CRI-O.

flashcards.question: What is a Kubernetes Object, and what do its two fields mean?
flashcards.answer: A persistent entity representing the state of the cluster - it can describe what containerized applications are running and on which nodes, the resources available to those applications, and the policies governing their behaviour (restart policies, upgrades, fault tolerance). It is a 'record of intent': once created, the system constantly works to ensure that object exists. Its two fields are spec (the desired state, e.g. 3 instances of the application) and status (the current state, e.g. 2 instances currently up and running).

flashcards.question: Explain how a Deployment object demonstrates the spec/status reconciliation loop.
flashcards.answer: You create the Deployment with a spec of, say, 3 replicas. Kubernetes reads the spec, starts 3 instances, and updates the status to match. If one instance fails - a status change - Kubernetes responds to the difference between spec and status by starting a replacement instance. Nothing decided this ahead of time; the loop simply keeps closing the gap.

flashcards.question: What is a Pod?
flashcards.answer: The smallest deployable unit of computing that can be created and managed in Kubernetes. It models an application-specific 'logical host' containing one or more relatively tightly-coupled application containers that share storage resources, network resources, and a specification for how to run. Its contents are always co-located and co-scheduled and run in a shared context - a set of Linux namespaces and cgroups plus shared filesystem volumes - though individual applications inside a Pod may have further sub-isolations applied.

flashcards.question: What is a Kubernetes Node, and how does one join a cluster?
flashcards.answer: A virtual or physical machine that is a member of the cluster; each node is managed by the control plane and contains the services necessary to run Pods. A node joins in one of two ways: the kubelet on the node self-registers to the control plane (API server), or a human user manually adds a Node object to the API server. Once created, the control plane checks whether the Node object is valid - all services running - before it can run a Pod.

flashcards.question: What describes a Kubernetes Node's status?
flashcards.answer: Address: hostname and internal/external IP. Conditions: parameters like ready, disk pressure, memory pressure, PID pressure, network unavailable - if the ready status is false or unknown for too long, the node controller triggers eviction of all Pods assigned to that node. Capacity: the resources available on the node - CPU, memory, and the maximum number of pods that can be scheduled onto it. Allocable: the amount of resources actually available to be consumed by normal Pods. Info: general information such as kernel version, Kubernetes version, container runtime details, and operating system.

flashcards.question: What is a Kubernetes controller, and how does the Job controller illustrate it?
flashcards.answer: Controllers are control loops that watch the state of the cluster and make or request changes where needed. A controller tracks at least one resource type, comparing its spec (desired state) against the current state and trying to bring them together via messages to the API server. Job controller example: a Job runs one or more Pods to carry out a task and then stop. When the Job controller sees a new Job, it ensures the kubelets on a set of Nodes are running the right number of Pods to get the work done, telling the API server to create or remove Pods. Once the work is done, it updates the Job object to mark it Finished.

flashcards.question: How does a controller that manages external resources differ, using the Cluster Autoscaler as the example?
flashcards.answer: Most controllers only create and remove Kubernetes objects internally. A controller managing external state - like the Cluster Autoscaler, which ensures there are enough Nodes in the cluster and can horizontally scale them - reads its desired state from the API server but then communicates directly with an external system to bring the current state in line.

flashcards.question: How does the kube-scheduler decide where to place a Pod?
flashcards.answer: In two stages. Filtering: it finds the set of Nodes where it's feasible to schedule the Pod - e.g. the PodFitsResources filter checks whether a candidate Node has enough available resources for the Pod's requests; it can explore all cluster nodes or only a subset. The result is the list of feasible nodes; if empty, the Pod isn't schedulable and remains pending until the scheduler can place it. Scoring: it assigns a score to each surviving Node using the active scoring rules, then assigns the Pod to the highest-ranking Node, choosing at random among ties. It then notifies the API server of the decision, in a process called binding. Factors considered include individual and collective resource requirements, hardware/software/policy constraints, affinity and anti-affinity specifications, data locality, inter-workload interference, and deadlines.

flashcards.question: What is Node Affinity, and what is it useful for?
flashcards.answer: A set of rules specifying preferences that affect how Pods are placed on Nodes - it constrains which nodes a Pod is eligible to be scheduled on, based on labels on the nodes and whether those labels match the rules. Useful for: ensuring compliance with data sovereignty laws (e.g. forcing pods onto European nodes); optimizing network latency for distributed systems (co-locating pods that communicate heavily); allocating resources for high-performance computing (forcing a pod onto GPU nodes); handling workloads with specific storage requirements (e.g. a database needing SSD nodes); and supporting multi-tenancy and resource isolation (dedicating specific nodes to a specific customer).

flashcards.question: What is Node Anti-Affinity, and what is it useful for?
flashcards.answer: Rules that prevent the scheduling of pods on the same node (or on specific nodes). Particularly useful for high-availability deployments, where spreading pods across different nodes or zones reduces the risk of a single point of failure, and for separating workloads that shouldn't be co-located for performance or security reasons.

flashcards.question: What forms of data storage exist in cloud systems, and what goals do they share?
flashcards.answer: Distributed file systems (GFS, HDFS - building on concepts from network and parallel file systems), SQL databases, and NoSQL databases/datastores including key-value stores like BigTable and Dynamo. Common goals: massive scaling on demand, high availability, and simplified application development and deployment.

flashcards.question: What is an atomic transaction, and what does atomicity require?
flashcards.answer: A multi-step operation that should complete without any interruption. It requires hardware support in the form of non-interruptable ISA operations - test-and-set, which writes to a memory location and returns the old content as a single non-interruptible operation, and compare-and-swap, which compares a memory location's contents to a given value and modifies it only if the two match. It also requires mechanisms to access shared resources and create critical sections: locks, semaphores, monitors.

flashcards.question: Describe all-or-nothing atomicity.
flashcards.answer: A multi-step operation either fully completes or leaves no trace. Pre-commit phase: preparatory actions that can still be undone - allocating a resource, fetching a page from secondary storage, allocating memory on the stack. Transition: the commit point. Post-commit phase: irreversible actions, e.g. altering the only copy of an object or writing to a file. Maintaining a history of changes and a log of actions is what allows the system to deal with failures and consistency.

flashcards.question: Describe before-or-after atomicity.
flashcards.answer: The result of every read or write is the same as if that read or write occurred either completely before or completely after any other read or write. So for a sequence of reads and writes in a transaction, concurrent execution must produce the same result as executing them serialized.

flashcards.question: What is the cell storage model, and what does it guarantee?
flashcards.answer: It assumes memory is organized into cells of the same size, with each object fitting exactly in one cell; the read/write unit is a sector or block. It reflects the physical organization of common storage media - primary memory as an array of cells, secondary storage as sectors or blocks. It guarantees read/write coherence (a read of a cell returns the most recent write to that cell) and before-or-after atomicity, but NOT all-or-nothing atomicity: once a cell's content is changed by an action there is no way to abort and restore the original.

flashcards.question: What is the journal storage model, and why does it guarantee all-or-nothing atomicity?
flashcards.answer: A model for storing complex objects such as multi-field records, consisting of a manager on top of cell storage. The user has no direct access to the cell storage - they ask the journal manager to start an action, read a cell, write a cell, commit, or abort, and the manager translates these into cell-storage commands. Unlike cell storage, it maintains the entire history of every variable in a log, not just the current value; each update is appended as a record, and the log is authoritative - the cell store can be reconstructed from it. An all-or-nothing action first records the action in the log, then installs the change in cell storage. If either operation fails the action is discarded (abort), which is exactly what cell storage alone can't do. And all-or-nothing atomicity implies both read/write coherence and before-or-after atomicity.

flashcards.question: Why are relational DBMSs a poor fit for many cloud applications, and what do NoSQL databases trade away?
flashcards.answer: Relational DBMSs guarantee ACID (atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability) and are reliable, but they are usually not scalable in speed or size - they struggle with massive data volumes under real-time demands, and horizontal scaling raises the problem of consistency across distributed copies. Memcaching (keeping objects in RAM) mitigates response time but not the underlying scalability issue. NoSQL databases drop the relational model and the ACID guarantee in favour of BASE - basically available, soft state, eventually consistent - designed to scale horizontally and eliminate the single point of failure, pushing responsibility for ACID-like behaviour onto the application developer.

flashcards.question: What structural properties let NoSQL and document stores scale and stay resilient?
flashcards.answer: They are designed to scale well, exhibit no single point of failure, have built-in support for consensus-based decisions taken on the majority of votes, and support partitioning and replication as basic primitives. Data will be eventually consistent at some future point rather than being made consistent at commit time - and both partitioning and replication increase availability, reduce response time, and enhance scalability.

flashcards.question: What is consensus, and what do consensus protocols guarantee - and not guarantee?
flashcards.answer: Consensus is the process of agreeing on one of several alternatives proposed by a number of agents; in distributed systems the agents are processes expected to reach agreement on a single proposed value. Consensus protocols guarantee freedom from inconsistencies - safety. They do NOT guarantee fault tolerance, and they cannot guarantee progress. Paxos is a family of consensus protocols based on a finite state machine approach, and it is core to supporting eventual consistency in NoSQL datastores.

flashcards.question: What is a Byzantine failure, and what are its two categories?
flashcards.answer: A failure with no restriction on what errors can be created - a failed node can generate arbitrary data, including data that makes it look like a functioning node to a subset of other nodes. Omission failures: crash failure, or failure to receive a request or send a response. Commission failures: processing a request incorrectly, corrupting local state, or sending an incorrect or inconsistent response. Paxos assumes agents and the network may fail, but NOT Byzantine failures.

flashcards.question: What are the roles in basic Paxos, and what are a quorum and a proposal?
flashcards.answer: Client: issues a request and waits for a response. Proposer: advocates a client's request and convinces the acceptors to agree on the value. Leader: a distinguished proposer that acts as coordinator to move the protocol forward in case of conflicts. Acceptor: the fault-tolerant 'memory' of the protocol. Learner: the replication factor - it takes action once a request has been agreed upon. A quorum is a subset of all acceptors; a proposal is a pair (unique proposal number, proposed value), and a value is chosen once a simple majority of acceptors have accepted it. In a typical deployment one node plays proposer, acceptor, and learner at once.

flashcards.question: Walk through the two phases of basic Paxos.
flashcards.answer: Phase I - Prepare and Promise: the proposer sends a prepare message with proposal number k to a majority of acceptors, requesting that no proposal numbered below k be accepted, and asking for the highest-numbered proposal each has already accepted. An acceptor must remember the highest proposal number it ever accepted and the highest it ever responded to; it can accept proposal k only if it hasn't already responded to a prepare request numbered above k - otherwise it doesn't reply (lost messages are treated the same as choosing not to respond). Phase II - Accept request and Accept: if a majority responds, the proposer chooses the value of the highest-numbered proposal among the responses, or an arbitrary value if none was issued, and sends an accept request (k, v) to a quorum. An acceptor accepts it only if it hasn't since promised to consider a proposal numbered above k; if it accepts, it registers v and notifies the proposer and every learner, otherwise it ignores the request.

flashcards.question: What assumptions about GFS's access model shaped its design?
flashcards.answer: Files range from a few GB to hundreds of TB; workloads append rather than randomly write; reads are sequential; response time is not the main requirement since data is processed in bulk; and a relaxed consistency model is acceptable, simplifying the implementation without placing an additional burden on application developers. The design also assumes high reliability is needed in the face of frequent hardware failures, software errors, and human errors, since the system is built from thousands of inexpensive commodity components.

flashcards.question: Why does GFS use 64MB chunks instead of the 0.5-2MB blocks of a normal filesystem?
flashcards.answer: To optimize performance for large files and reduce the metadata the master must maintain; to increase the likelihood that multiple operations are directed to the same chunk, which reduces the number of requests needed to locate a chunk and lets the client maintain a persistent network connection to that chunkserver; and to reduce disk fragmentation. The tradeoff is that a chunk for a small file, or the last chunk of a large file, is only partially filled.

flashcards.question: Describe the GFS cluster architecture and how the master survives failure.
flashcards.answer: A single master controls a large number of chunkservers and maintains metadata: filenames, access control information, the location of all replicas for every chunk, and the state of individual chunkservers. Chunk locations are stored only in the master's memory and are rebuilt at startup or when a new chunkserver joins - which is how the master always has up-to-date location information. For recovery, an operation log maintains a historical record of metadata changes; changes are atomic and are not made visible to clients until recorded on multiple replicas on persistent storage. To minimize recovery time the master periodically checkpoints its state, replaying only the log records after the last checkpoint.

flashcards.question: Who handles file creation versus file access in GFS?
flashcards.answer: File creation is handled by the master. File access is handled between the application and the chunkserver: the master grants a lease to one chunkserver - the primary - which is then responsible for handling the updates.

flashcards.question: Walk through a GFS write.
flashcards.answer: 1) The client contacts the master, which assigns a lease to one chunkserver (the primary) and replies with the IDs of the primary and secondary chunkservers. 2) The client sends the data to all chunkservers, which store it in an LRU buffer and send an ACK back. 3) The client sends the write request to the primary, which applies the mutation to the file. 4) The primary forwards the write to the secondaries. 5) Each secondary ACKs the primary once the mutation is applied. 6) The primary ACKs the client.

flashcards.question: Describe the HDFS architecture.
flashcards.answer: A master/slave architecture. The NameNode (master) manages the filesystem namespace, stores metadata, controls access to files, records changes in a log, and checks DataNode liveness; a Secondary NameNode supports high availability. The DataNodes (slaves) handle the low-level read/write operations. It replicates data across multiple nodes - three replicas by default - distributing a large dataset over many nodes.

flashcards.question: What are HDFS's basic technical properties, and what are the three stages of an HDFS write?
flashcards.answer: It is a distributed filesystem written in Java, portable but not directly mountable on an existing OS, not fully POSIX compliant but highly performant, using 64-128MB blocks with three replicas by default. A write has three stages: setup of the pipeline, data streaming and replication (the write pipeline stage itself), and shutdown of the pipeline (the acknowledgement stage).

flashcards.question: Describe Amazon S3's storage model.
flashcards.answer: Data is stored as named objects grouped into named buckets - essentially a map of bucket + key + version. Buckets have global names, must be explicitly created via the API before use, can be listed and deleted, and use ACLs to set read/write permissions, with a per-user limit on how many you can create. An object can store any byte string up to a few GB, has a key unique within its bucket, and is identified by a URI path name. Objects are stored and retrieved either in their entirety or by specific byte ranges, with PUT and GET.

flashcards.question: How does S3 handle read and write failures?
flashcards.answer: The application must handle them - the client must retry a request until it succeeds. Integrity is checked with an MD5 checksum returned in the ETag field. On write: S3 computes the MD5 of every object written and returns it in the ETag as part of the write acknowledgment; the client is supposed to compute the MD5 of the object it wrote and compare - if the two don't match, the object was corrupted in transmission or storage and should be re-written. On read: S3 returns the object's MD5 in the ETag; if it doesn't match the value the client computes on the received data, a read error occurred and the client retries.

flashcards.question: What is S3's data consistency model for objects?
flashcards.answer: Strong read-after-write consistency for PUT and DELETE requests of objects - both new objects and PUTs overwriting existing ones - and strong consistency for reads on access control lists, object tags, and object metadata. Updates to a single key are atomic: a PUT from one thread with a concurrent GET from another returns either the old data or the new data, never partial or corrupt data. A PUT returns successfully only after all replicas are updated.

flashcards.question: What are S3's limits around concurrency and multi-key operations?
flashcards.answer: S3 does not support object locking for concurrent writers - if two PUT requests are made simultaneously to the same key, the request with the latest timestamp wins (last-writer-wins). If that's a problem, the programmer must build an object-locking mechanism into the application. Updates are also key-based with no way to make atomic updates across keys: you cannot make the update of one key depend on the update of another unless the client application implements it. Since the order in which S3 receives requests and the order applications receive acknowledgments cannot be predicted, the best way to determine the final value is to read after both writes have been acknowledged.

flashcards.question: What is S3's consistency model for buckets, as opposed to objects?
flashcards.answer: Bucket configurations follow an eventual consistency model, unlike objects. After deleting a bucket and immediately listing all buckets, the deleted one might still appear. Enabling versioning on a bucket for the first time may take a short time to fully propagate - so a waiting period is suggested before performing PUT/DELETE requests on objects after such a change.

flashcards.question: Describe BigTable's data model.
flashcards.answer: A BigTable is a sparse, distributed, persistent, multidimensional sorted map, indexed by a row key, a column key, and a timestamp - (row:string, column:string, time:int64) mapping to a string. Each value is an uninterpreted array of bytes. It supports dynamic control over data layout and format: clients control the locality properties of their data through an appropriate schema, and can control whether data is served from memory or disk. Row and column names can be arbitrary strings.

flashcards.question: How do rows, column families, and timestamps work in BigTable?
flashcards.answer: Rows: a row key is an arbitrary string up to 64KB; read/write on a single row is atomic regardless of how many columns are involved; rows are ordered lexicographically and a row range is dynamically partitioned into tablets, the unit of load balancing - so reads of short row ranges are efficient, touching only a few machines, and clients exploit this for locality (e.g. Webtable reverses the hostname so pages from one site cluster together). Column families: column keys are grouped into families, all data in a family shares the same type and can be compressed, a family must be created before data is stored under any of its keys, families should be few and rarely changed while the number of columns per family is unbounded, and access control and disk/memory accounting are done at family level - a column key is written family:qualifier. Timestamps: each cell can hold multiple versions indexed by a 64-bit timestamp, assigned by BigTable (real time in microseconds) or by the client (which must then avoid collisions itself); versions are stored newest-first, and garbage collection keeps either only the last n versions or only versions newer than n days.

flashcards.question: What are BigTable's building blocks, and what is Chubby used for?
flashcards.answer: Three building blocks: the Google File System for storage, the Google SSTable file format, and Chubby. Chubby is a lock service for loosely-coupled distributed systems, allowing clients to synchronize their activities and agree on basic information about their environment, based on files and directories. BigTable uses it to ensure there is at most one active master at any time, to store the bootstrap location of BigTable data, to discover tablet servers and finalize tablet server deaths, to store schema information, and to store access control lists. GFS uses Chubby too, but more narrowly - to elect the master server and store a small amount of metadata.

flashcards.question: How does Dynamo partition data, and what problem do virtual nodes solve?
flashcards.answer: Dynamo dynamically partitions data over a set of nodes to scale incrementally, using consistent hashing: each node is assigned a random value representing its position on a ring, and each data item is assigned to a node by hashing its key to a position on that same ring. The challenge is non-uniform distribution and load imbalance. Virtual nodes solve it: a physical node holds multiple positions on the ring rather than one. If a node becomes unavailable its load is evenly dispersed across the remaining nodes; when it returns or a new node is added, it accepts a roughly equivalent amount of load from each of the others. The number of virtual nodes a node holds can be set according to its capacity, accounting for heterogeneity in the physical infrastructure.

flashcards.question: Why is Dynamo eventually consistent, and what does optimistic replication introduce?
flashcards.answer: Synchronous replica coordination leads to poor data availability, so Dynamo instead allows changes to propagate to replicas asynchronously in the background - all updates reach all replicas eventually. A read/write succeeds once a minimum number of nodes complete it successfully. This increases availability in a failure-prone environment, but optimistic replication introduces conflicting changes that must be detected and resolved. Dynamo's answer: always writable, with conflicts resolved at READ time - resolved either by the data store (limited logic) or by the application (more complex but more flexible). Each modification is treated as a new immutable version, and vector clocks (node, counter) mark versions and capture causality.

flashcards.question: Use Case 1 (video conference system): is it a cloud service? Justify against each essential characteristic.
flashcards.answer: Yes - all five hold. On-demand self-service: Option A, fully automated provisioning (resources allocated automatically, max ~2 min, transparent to consumers). Broad network access: Option A, available over the Internet via an HTTPS front-end website and a standard browser. Resource pooling: the same video conference software platform serves all users via a multi-tenant model, and the physical/virtual resources running those instances are shared among CSCs. Rapid elasticity: Option A, automated and near-real-time - if participants increase unpredictably, more resources are allocated automatically with a 1-60 second setup time. Measured service: a monitoring platform measures data traffic, computing resources per conference, and storage per user, supporting QoS guarantees and billing.

flashcards.question: Use Case 2 (scientific computing platform): is it a cloud service, and precisely which characteristics fail?
flashcards.answer: No. It fails On-demand self-service (a back-office of human operators processes every VM instantiation request, verifying physical capacity and queueing if none is available), fails Rapid elasticity (requests are only guaranteed served within 36 hours of checkout), and fails Measured service in substance (usage is measured, but the CSC pays for instantiated resources even if unused). Broad network access and Resource pooling do hold. Failing even one characteristic disqualifies it.
